summary: DeVault distills software engineering into four fundamental principles: robustness, reliability, stability, and simplicity. He defines each concisely — robustness means handling all known edge cases, reliability means working for extended periods without failure, stability means not changing in incompatible ways, and simplicity means minimizing moving parts. He emphasizes that simplicity serves the other three goals and that designing simple solutions is harder than designing complex ones.
key_points: Robustness requires predicting and handling all known error cases and reasoning about performance characteristics
Stability demands advance notice and stakeholder involvement when changes are necessary
Simplicity is the meta-principle: only include as many moving parts as needed to meet the other three goals
Designing a simple solution is usually harder than designing a complex one
money_quotes: Software should be robust. It should be designed to accommodate all known edge cases.
It should be expected to work for an extended length of time under design conditions without failures.
It should not change in incompatible or unexpected ways; if it works today it should also work tomorrow.
If it has to change, a plan shall be written. Stakeholders (including users!) should be given advance notice and should be involved in the planning stage.
Only as many moving parts should be included as necessary to meet the other three goals.
All software has bugs, but complicated software (1) has more bugs and (2) is more difficult to diagnose and fix.
Note that designing a simple solution is usually more difficult than designing a complex solution.
key_insight: Good software engineering reduces to four principles — robustness, reliability, stability, and simplicity — where simplicity is the hardest to achieve and the most important because it enables the other three.
